title: Constructors & Static Members
description: Master constructor overloading, chaining with this() and super(), and the static keyword — the trio of concepts that appear in virtually every CSE110 OOP exam question involving object initialisation and shared class-level state.

sections.content: 🎓 Why Constructors & Static Matter in CSE110 Exams

Two of the most reliably tested OOP concepts in CSE110 are: **what order do constructors execute?** (especially when chaining with `this()` or involving inheritance with `super()`) and **what does a static field's value change to after a sequence of object creations?** If you can trace constructor execution order and distinguish static from instance state, you will handle every predict-output and find-error question in this domain correctly.
🏗️ Constructors: Rules and Types

A **constructor** is a special method that runs automatically when an object is created with `new`. It initialises the object's fields. Constructors have no return type (not even `void`) and must have the **exact same name as the class**. A class may have multiple constructors as long as they differ in parameter lists — this is **constructor overloading**. If you write no constructor at all, Java silently provides a **default no-argument constructor** that initialises all fields to their defaults. The moment you write **any** constructor, Java no longer provides the default one.

rows: Empty body; initialises fields to defaults (0, false, null)
Only if NO constructor is written by programmer
Programmer writes `ClassName() { ... }` — can set custom defaults
Programmer defined; must be explicit if any overloaded constructor exists
Programmer defined (Java has no automatic copy constructor)

sections.title: Constructor Overloading — Car Example

sections.label: Exam Trap: The Disappearing Default Constructor
sections.content: If you write **any** constructor in your class, Java removes the implicit no-argument constructor. Code that previously compiled — `new Car()` — will fail with a **compile-time error**: `"constructor Car() is undefined."` This surprises many students when they add their first parameterised constructor. Fix: explicitly write a no-arg constructor if you need one alongside your parameterised constructors.
🔗 Constructor Chaining with this()

Instead of duplicating initialisation code across multiple overloaded constructors, one constructor can call another using `this(arguments)`. This is called **constructor chaining**. The `this()` call must be the **very first statement** in the constructor body — no other code may appear before it. The call delegates to whichever constructor in the same class matches the argument types.

sections.title: Constructor Chaining with this() — DRY Initialisation

sections.label: Constructor Chaining Execution Order Is Bottom-Up
sections.content: When you chain `A → B → C` (A calls this() to B, B calls this() to C), the execution order of constructor **bodies** is C, then B, then A — deepest first. The `this()` call transfers control immediately, so any `System.out.println` you put **after** the `this()` call runs only after the entire chain below has completed. This is a classic predict-output trap.
🔢 Static Fields

A **static field** (also called a class variable) belongs to the **class itself**, not to any individual object. There is exactly one copy of a static field in memory, shared by all instances of the class. Common uses: object counters (how many instances have been created), shared constants, and caches. Static fields are initialised once when the class is first loaded — before any object is created.

sections.title: Static Field — Object Instance Counter

sections.content: ⚙️ Static Methods

A **static method** belongs to the class, not to any object. It has no implicit `this` parameter. Static methods can only directly access **other static members** — they cannot refer to instance fields or call instance methods without first having an object reference. The classic use case: utility methods (`Math.sqrt`, `Integer.parseInt`) and factory methods that create objects.

sections.title: Static Methods — Rules and Patterns

sections.label: Exam Trap: Accessing Instance Members from static main()
sections.content: `public static void main(String[] args)` is itself a static method. You **cannot** directly call instance methods or access instance fields from `main` without first creating an object. `System.out.println(name)` inside `main` will give a compile-time error: `"non-static variable name cannot be referenced from a static context."` This is the single most common compile-time error in CSE110. Fix: `MyClass obj = new MyClass(); System.out.println(obj.name);`
📊 Static vs Instance: Complete Comparison

rows: Class is first loaded by JVM

sections.content: 🔄 Static Initialisation and Constants

Static fields can be initialised at declaration, in a **static initialisation block** `static { ... }`, or in a static method. The `static { }` block runs once when the class is first loaded — before `main`, before any constructor. Combine `static final` to declare **named constants** — values that are shared across all instances and cannot be reassigned.

sections.title: Static Initialisation Block and Constants

sections.label: Initialisation Order: static block → main() → constructors
sections.content: The JVM loads the class, runs all `static { }` blocks in order, then enters `main()`. Only after that do constructor calls happen. The order is: **static initialisation → main() body → constructor(s)**. This affects output order in predict-output questions involving static blocks.
🧩 Common Constructor Patterns in Exams

rows: Fields get default values (0, false, null)
Prints in bottom-up order; this() must be first
Can never be reassigned; shared by all
Need an object: `new Foo().go()` or `Foo f = new Foo(); f.go();`

sections.content: 🏋️ Practice Exercises

sections.title: Exercise 1: Tracing Constructor Chaining Output Order
sections.answer:
Lamp(color,brightness,isOn) called
Lamp(color,brightness) called
Lamp() called
white 50 false
sections.explanation:
Execution trace:

1. `new Lamp()` → enters `Lamp()`, first statement is `this("white", 50)` — control passes to `Lamp(String, int)`.
2. Inside `Lamp(String, int)`, first statement is `this("white", 50, false)` — control passes to `Lamp(String, int, boolean)`.
3. Inside `Lamp(String, int, boolean)`: fields are set (`color="white"`, `brightness=50`, `isOn=false`), then **prints** `"Lamp(color,brightness,isOn) called"`. This constructor returns.
4. Back in `Lamp(String, int)`: the `this(...)` call has returned, so the next statement **prints** `"Lamp(color,brightness) called"`. This constructor returns.
5. Back in `Lamp()`: the `this(...)` call has returned, so the next statement **prints** `"Lamp() called"`. This constructor returns.
6. `main` continues: `a.color` = "white", `a.brightness` = 50, `a.isOn` = false → prints `"white 50 false"`.

**Key rule:** `this()` transfers control immediately; everything below it executes only after the called constructor finishes — so the deepest constructor always prints first.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Static Counter Across Multiple Object Creations
sections.explanation:
**`nextId` is static** — one shared counter for all Ticket objects.

Initially: `nextId = 1`.

`new Ticket("Concert")`: t1.id = 1, then nextId becomes 2.
`new Ticket("Festival")`: t2.id = 2, then nextId becomes 3.

**Line 1:** `t1.id + " " + t2.id` → `"1 2"`
**Line 2:** `Ticket.nextId` → `3`

`new Ticket("Theatre")`: t3.id = 3, then nextId becomes 4.
**Line 3:** `t3.id` → `3`
**Line 4:** `t1.nextId` — accessing static field via instance reference; same as `Ticket.nextId` → `4`

`new Ticket("Opera")`: nextId is 4, so this new Ticket.id = 4, then nextId becomes 5. Variable `t1` is reassigned to point to this new object.
**Line 5:** `t1.id` → `4` (the new object's id)

sections.title: Exercise 3: Accessing Instance Member from Static Context
sections.error: Line 10: Compile-time error — `"non-static variable radius cannot be referenced from a static context."` The instance field `radius` belongs to objects, but `main` is a static method and has no `this` reference. There is no object, so `radius` is undefined inside `main`.
sections.explanation:
`main` is a `static` method — it belongs to the class, not to any object. Instance fields like `radius` only exist when an object has been created with `new`. Inside a static context, Java has no `this` to look up, so `radius` is inaccessible.

The fix has two parts: (1) move the area calculation into an **instance method** `area()`, where `this` is available and `radius` makes sense; (2) create a `Circle` object in `main` and call the method on it.

This is a **compile-time error** — the code will not run at all until the reference to `radius` is removed from the static context.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Missing No-Arg Constructor After Adding Parameterised One
sections.error: Line 11: Compile-time error — `"constructor Player() in class Player cannot be applied to given types; no suitable constructor found."` Once the programmer defines `Player(String, int)`, Java no longer generates the implicit no-argument constructor, so `new Player()` fails.
sections.explanation:
Java automatically provides a **default no-arg constructor** only when the programmer has written **zero constructors**. The moment `Player(String, int)` was added, the default constructor disappeared.

The fix explicitly defines `Player()` with sensible default values. Alternatively, the no-arg constructor could use constructor chaining: `Player() { this("Unknown", 0); }` — this delegates to the parameterised constructor and avoids duplicating the assignment logic.

This is a **compile-time error** — caught by the compiler before execution.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Design a Library Book Tracker with Static Count
sections.approach: Use a static field `totalBooks` initialised to 0. In the constructor, increment totalBooks first, then assign bookId = totalBooks. This gives IDs starting at 1. The static method getTotal() simply returns totalBooks. toString() formats the output string.
sections.explanation:
**Static field `totalBooks`:** shared across all instances. Starts at 0. Each constructor call increments it before using it as the new book's ID — this guarantees IDs are 1, 2, 3, ... in creation order.

**Static method `getTotal()`:** accesses only `totalBooks` (a static field) — no instance needed. Called as `Book.getTotal()`.

**`toString()` override:** Java calls this automatically when a Book object is passed to `println` or concatenated with a String, producing the formatted output line.

**Design insight:** `totalBooks` also serves as the next available ID because we increment it **before** assigning. If we assigned first and incremented after, IDs would start at 0 — a common subtle bug in this pattern.

sections.content: 📝 Quick Reference Summary

items: **Constructor** = special method with no return type, same name as class; runs automatically on `new`.
**Default constructor** (no-arg, empty body) is provided by Java **only if no constructor is written**. Adding any constructor removes it.
**Constructor overloading:** multiple constructors in one class, each with a different parameter list.
**`this(args)`** calls another constructor in the same class — must be the **very first** statement; chains execute deepest-first.
**Static field:** one shared copy per class, not per object. Persists for the lifetime of the program. Accessed as `ClassName.field`.
**Static method:** no implicit `this`; can only directly access static members. Instance fields/methods require an object reference.
**`static final`** = named constant: shared across all instances, can never be reassigned after initialisation. Use UPPER_SNAKE_CASE.
**Static initialisation block** `static { }` runs once when the class is first loaded — before `main()` and before any constructor.

**Common compile error:** `"non-static variable X cannot be referenced from a static context"` — you accessed an instance member inside `main` or another static method without creating an object first.
